⭐ Vendor of the Week

The Trade Desk Vets Building the Bloomberg Terminal for AI Search

Brian Stempeck and his co-founders scaled a $50 billion adtech company. Now they're betting that GEO — Generative Engine Optimization — will become the most important channel no PMM is thinking about yet.

By Chris O'Hara · March 2026 · 10 min read

Here's a scenario that should make every product marketer uncomfortable. A VP of Procurement at a Fortune 500 company opens ChatGPT and types: "What are the best human capital management platforms for a global enterprise?" The model names five vendors. Your company isn't one of them. No one on your team knows this happened. No one on your team knows it's been happening — thousands of times a day — across ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Perplexity, and Meta AI. And unlike a bad Google ranking, there's no page two where you might still show up. You're simply not in the conversation.

That's the problem Brian Stempeck saw coming before most marketers had even started asking the question. And the company he built to solve it — Evertune — is quietly becoming one of the most interesting vendors in the martech landscape precisely because it addresses a gap that didn't exist eighteen months ago.

The Origin Story Nobody Expected

Brian Stempeck's career reads like a choose-your-own-adventure that somehow always lands in the right chapter. He started as a political journalist on Capitol Hill, covering energy policy for E&E Publishing — the kind of beat where you learn to extract signal from noise while Senators try to spin you. He launched their online video division, went to business school at UNC Kenan-Flagler, won a global innovation competition judged by American Express and Shell, did a stint at Bain advising pharma and retail companies on digital strategy, then managed the Dove brand at Unilever.

None of that, on paper, qualifies you to be employee number eight at a programmatic advertising startup. But when Stempeck joined The Trade Desk in 2010, the company had seven employees and no revenue. Over the next eleven-plus years, he'd open their New York office, build it into the company's largest location, serve as Chief Client Officer, then Chief Strategy Officer, earn a board seat, and help grow the thing from zero to a $50 billion S&P 500 company with an IPO that actually changed how Wall Street thought about adtech. He didn't just have a front-row seat to one of the great enterprise scaling stories in digital advertising — he was one of the people building the stage.

Which is exactly why the founding story of Evertune feels so inevitable in hindsight. In early 2024, Stempeck was reconnecting with two former Trade Desk colleagues — Ed Chater and Poul Costinsky, both of whom had come to TTD through the acquisition of AdBrain, a cross-device identity graph company they'd co-founded. The three of them kept arriving at the same observation: they were using ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude to make real purchasing decisions. Brian needed a pool heater. He went to the LLMs instead of Google. And what he found — the wild inconsistency across models, the opaque logic behind recommendations, the total absence of measurement tools — looked exactly like the early days of programmatic advertising, before anyone had built the infrastructure to make it work.

They launched Evertune in April 2024. By August 2025, they'd closed a $15 million Series A led by Felicis Ventures, with angel investors from OpenAI, Meta, and Uber backing the vision. The company now has about forty people in New York and works with enterprise brands including Canada Goose, Miro, and WPP's Choreograph.

What GEO Actually Is (and Why PMMs Should Care)

Let's get the terminology straight, because the space is still arguing about what to call itself. GEO — Generative Engine Optimization — is the practice of measuring and improving how your brand shows up when people ask AI models for recommendations, comparisons, and purchasing advice. Some people call it Answer Engine Optimization. Others call it AI Search Visibility. The name hasn't settled yet, which tells you something about how young this category is.

But here's what has settled: the behavioral shift is real, it's massive, and it's accelerating. AI-referred sessions jumped over 500% in the first half of 2025 alone. Forrester reports that 89% of B2B buyers now use generative AI as a key source of self-guided information during their purchasing journey. And when someone asks an LLM a question, it typically cites only two to seven domains in its response — compared to Google's ten blue links. The competition for those few citation slots is ferocious, and most brands don't even know they're losing it.

For PMMs specifically, the implications are profound. All that messaging work, all those carefully crafted positioning documents and competitive narratives — they were built for a world where humans read your content and made decisions. Now there's an intermediary. The LLMs are reading your content — along with your competitors', and analyst reports, and Reddit threads, and G2 reviews — and synthesizing all of it into a single recommendation. The question isn't just whether your content is good. It's whether it's structured, authoritative, and distributed in ways that AI models can find, interpret, and cite.

Inside Evertune's Platform

Evertune's approach starts with measurement at a scale that borders on obsessive. They run over one million prompts per brand per month — sampling each prompt a hundred times across models to generate statistically significant results. Most competing platforms sample each prompt once per model per day, which is the AI-search equivalent of checking your stock price once a quarter and calling it a trading strategy.

1M+ prompts per brand per month
100x sampling per prompt for statistical significance
8 AI models monitored (ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Perplexity, Meta AI, DeepSeek, Copilot, AI Overviews)

The platform breaks into four main capabilities. First, there's AI Brand Monitoring — think of it as share of voice for the LLM era. Evertune's "AI Brand Index" measures the probability that a model will recommend your brand unprompted across six dimensions: top of mind, brand perception, media effectiveness, price value, customer service, and product quality. They also track word association and sentiment — what adjectives and phrases do the models consistently attach to your brand versus competitors? — which is the kind of perception data that would take months to gather through traditional market research.

Second, there's Consumer Preferences. This is where it gets interesting for PMMs. Evertune identifies which buyer attributes — say, "scalability" or "ease of implementation" or "total cost of ownership" — actually drive model recommendations in your category. If ChatGPT consistently recommends Competitor X when users ask about integration flexibility, but your brand owns the "enterprise security" attribute, you now have a data-driven content roadmap that tells you exactly where to invest.

Third, Content Strategy. Evertune's Content Analytics reveal which specific domains and URLs are most influencing the models' perceptions in your category. Not all third-party coverage is equal — some publisher domains carry dramatically more weight in how LLMs form their recommendations. The platform identifies those high-influence sites and — through a new feature called Partner Connect — lets you activate directly through affiliate platforms like Impact.com and PartnerStack to get your brand's story onto the pages that actually move the needle.

And fourth, AI Website Optimization — essentially a technical audit of whether the LLM crawlers can actually access, read, and index your content. Turns out a lot of enterprise websites are accidentally blocking AI bots entirely, which is like spending millions on a storefront and then boarding up the entrance.

The SAP View: What the Data Actually Shows

I got a firsthand look at Evertune's capabilities through an analysis they prepared for SAP's Human Capital Management category — the kind of teardown that makes you realize how little visibility most enterprises have into their AI search presence.

The analysis covered everything from how the models describe SAP (word association and sentiment analysis across ChatGPT, Gemini, and others) to how often the models recommend SAP on an unaided basis — meaning when nobody mentions SAP in the prompt. They measured competitive positioning across buyer preferences, identified the specific content topics SAP should prioritize to "educate" the models, and mapped the domains and URLs that carry the most weight in shaping AI perception for the HCM category.

What Evertune's Analysis Reveals

The platform generates what they call "AI Education Briefs" — tailored messaging recommendations that align a brand's strengths with language patterns that resonate with the models. Behind the scenes, Evertune creates roughly fifty candidate messages for each topic, runs them through the language models to identify which phrasings actually move the recommendation needle, and surfaces the top performers. It's A/B testing for LLM persuasion — the kind of thing that would have sounded like science fiction two years ago.

They also map which publishers allow AI models to train on their content versus just search it, versus blocking access entirely. That crawler-status data is gold for content distribution strategy, because it tells you which earned media placements will actually compound into long-term AI visibility versus evaporating after a single news cycle.

Case Studies That Tell the Story

The proof is in the results. A leading beauty brand came to Evertune capturing less than 25% of AI recommendations for cost-effective options in its category — the models were underselling them as a budget alternative rather than a premium-value leader. Evertune identified the messaging gap, built a framework around "premium ingredients at accessible prices" and "dermatologist-validated efficacy," and deployed it through seven strategic affiliate partnerships and four competitive comparison pieces on owned media. Within four months, the brand's Gemini recommendation share went from 24% to 69%, and ChatGPT from 23% to 38%.

In B2B software — closer to home for most of this newsletter's readers — a brand buried at rank fifteen in its category deployed Evertune's content strategy: eight strategic blog posts, product page overhauls focused on security and integration differentiators, and earned media placements across three tier-one industry publications. Within two months they'd cracked the top ten, with AI Brand Scores jumping 173% on Gemini and 105% on ChatGPT.

An automotive brand struggling with AI perception around safety saw similar results — an eighteen-point improvement in AI Brand Score for safety attributes and a jump from seventh to fifth place in category rankings, all within about a hundred days.

Why This Is a PMM Problem (Not Just an SEO Problem)

Here's where I want to get pointed, because I think most product marketing organizations are sleepwalking into a visibility crisis.

GEO is not SEO's little sibling. It's not something to hand off to the content team or the web optimization folks. The inputs that drive AI recommendations — messaging clarity, competitive differentiation, category authority, the distribution of narratives across earned and owned channels — these are the core outputs of product marketing. When an LLM decides whether to recommend your product, it's essentially running a miniature analyst evaluation based on the totality of what it's read about you. And the quality of that evaluation depends on whether your PMM team has done its job well enough for the models to understand your story.

Think about it through the Pragmatic Institute framework that many of us use. Positioning? The models are reading it. Competitive intelligence? They're synthesizing it. Sales enablement? The buyer might consult an LLM before they ever talk to your sales team. Pricing and packaging? Buyers are asking ChatGPT which vendors offer the best value. Every activity in the PMM discipline is now upstream of an AI model's recommendation engine — and most PMMs have zero visibility into how that engine is interpreting their work.

Five Things PMMs Should Do Right Now

Audit your AI visibility. Ask ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity some basic category questions — "What are the best [your category] solutions?" — and see where you land. Do it twenty times, because the responses vary. You'll probably be surprised.

Check your crawler access. Is your website blocking AI bots? Many enterprise sites do this accidentally through robots.txt configurations that were set up for a pre-LLM world.

Map your content for extractability. LLMs don't read your beautifully designed PDFs and gated whitepapers. They read structured HTML, published blog posts, ungated documentation, and third-party coverage. If your best content is trapped behind a form fill, the models will never see it.

Invest in earned media strategically. Not all press coverage is equal in the GEO world. The domains that LLMs cite most heavily are often different from the ones that drive the most web traffic. Your PR strategy needs a GEO lens.

Treat AI perception as a competitive intelligence discipline. Monitor it the way you monitor Gartner placements and G2 rankings. Build it into your quarterly competitive reviews. Because your competitors are starting to — and the brands that move first in GEO capture compounding advantages.

The Bigger Picture

What makes Evertune particularly interesting isn't just the platform — it's the founding team's pattern recognition. Stempeck, Chater, and Costinsky watched programmatic advertising go from a weird experiment that nobody understood to a category that reshaped the entire marketing industry. They built infrastructure at The Trade Desk while most of the market was still arguing about whether real-time bidding was even a real thing. And they're applying the same playbook now: build the measurement layer first, make the data actionable second, connect to activation channels third.

The GEO category is probably eighteen months old, depending on how you count. It doesn't even have a consensus name yet. The tools are evolving weekly. The models themselves are changing underneath everything. It's messy, it's early, and it's moving fast — which is exactly the kind of environment where the companies with enterprise-scale infrastructure DNA and a maniacal focus on data quality tend to win.

PMMs who are paying attention to this space now will have a meaningful head start. The ones who wait until their CMO asks "why aren't we showing up in ChatGPT?" will be playing catch-up in a game where the early movers have already trained the models to tell their story.

BS

Brian Stempeck

Brian is CEO & Co-Founder of Evertune. Previously Chief Strategy Officer and board member at The Trade Desk, where he was employee #8 and helped scale the company from zero to a $50B market cap. He started his career as a political journalist on Capitol Hill, holds an MBA from UNC Kenan-Flagler, and serves on the board of the Ad Council. Evertune is headquartered in New York City.

Are You Building in This Space?

We're looking for GEO tools, AI search analytics platforms, and content optimization vendors that PMMs should know about. If you're building something that helps product marketers navigate the AI discovery landscape, we want to hear from you.

Submit Your Vendor →

Congratulations to Brian Stempeck and the Evertune team for building what the GEO category clearly needs: not just another dashboard, but a genuine measurement-to-action platform that treats AI search as the channel it's rapidly becoming. The PMMs who figure this out early will have an enormous advantage — and Evertune is making that easier to do.
